## [00:00 - 03:01]

The vertical scaling strategy and how to scale confidently using this approach. The vertical scaling strategy is my preference when it comes to scaling accounts early on and being confident in scaling in a controlled way as well. So if you go back to the mindset training which hopefully you've had a look at the video, this is based on being in the top left quadrant of scaling confidently but not overly taking risks on your scaling. So if we go into the detail of this, the goal really for this scaling strategy is to scale up and reach more people, maintain your cost prequisition and your return on ad spend without overly risking this ad set competing with a duplicate ad set as we will discuss in the horizontal scaling strategy. The big advantage here is that you can maintain your ad history which has all the performance that you've already built up and then being able to compound your increments based on the goal of increasing your spend 20% at a time and we'll discuss shortly on how the approach is taken in terms of daily and how many times per day etc. There is a risk of unsettling your good performing ad set. It is also a slower scaling method but something which when you are early on in an ad set and it doesn't matter how many hundreds of thousands or thousands or millions you've already made, this is still a good strategy when you've got an ad set and it's a new product, new audience, new creative etc. and you want to scale and test the validity of your scaling as well. Generally from my practice I do 20% max per increment, maximum twice per day and I would only do that when you're absolutely confident in everything you're doing around the creative, the audience, the metrics etc. A 10% reduction as well, a performance does drop as well. From my experience of running multiple incremental strategies across different accounts, the 10% decrease tells the ad set that things are not going well and it's almost like a punishment signal for Facebook and it ends up in most cases improving performance. So if I'm increasing vertically on an ad set and I see a particular day that's not performing well when I expect it to be performing well, I'll do a decrement on that ad set for the budget and things will start to improve as well. Creative refresh is an important part of the vertical scaling strategy. So if you've been through the graduation framework and you've been through your creative vision, you will have creatives already in place to be able to insert into this ad set. Commonly when you are increasing your budgets vertically using the V scaling strategy, your frequencies may go up as your core audiences start to see more and more messages, but this is a good time to serve up new creatives just because they're not performing in terms of the higher spend with your core audience with the ad set that was working. It just may be that they need different messages. Either they've seen your message too many times or the

## [03:01 - 06:01]

good audience that's still there are just not responding to your previous message. So change up your messaging as you do start to increase your budgets vertically as well and keep an eye on audience and delivery insights in particular. So delivery insights is something I will discuss as part of this scaling strategy. Audience and delivery insights will kind of give you an insight as to whether you've already reached every single person in that audience or whether you're clashing with other ad sets. And it's a good way just to make sure that you are scaling confidently as well. And really the two key metrics you're looking out for with e-commerce when you're applying vertical scaling strategy is your cost-pracquisition and your return on ad spend. If you're running a digital product, if you're running lead gen, then it's cost-per-lead, cost-per-registration, for app install it's cost-per- install, cost-per-registration. So the key metrics are the ones that really matter for you. But keep an eye on the cost-per-click and click through it as well. So if you are scaling up quite commonly you'll also see your cost-per-click increasing as well as you start to go for potentially more lucrative options. And that's a key thing where you might want to consider your creative refresh, keep the message fresh to your new audiences as you start to reach out to them as well. And here on the right hand side you can see the impact, the compound impact of a 20% increase in budget. So 20% on $50 is $10 and so your budget becomes 60, but 10% or 60 your budget becomes 72. So you've increased 10 and $12 here and then 14 etc. And so the 20% strategy can increase your budgets and can be a kind of medium-paced opportunity for scaling and it's suitable especially when you're not confident completely on the product, the audience, creative etc. And you want a bit more control as well. When I am scaling vertically the methodology I use is the staircase scaling method. And so what this does is, and this is my best practice when it comes to testing a new account or a new business or a new ad set is to increment 20% every other day. And you do that until you're confident that things are scaling positively and then potentially you can be a lot more aggressive. If you are aggressive and this is what I commonly do, I will increase budgets 20% twice in a day. I will do it at the start of the day based on previous day's performance and I'll do it within the day if the metrics are really really good as well. Generally anywhere between kind of 12 and maybe 2-3 pm but no later because the later you increase your budgets the more chance that Facebook will waste your spend as well. So to give you a kind of really kind of edge case example, let's say you increase your budget 20% at 8 o'clock in the evening then chances are either Facebook will struggle to spend it or it will spend it but with a bad audience as well. So keep that in mind when you're scaling and then also thinking about

## [06:02 - 09:04]

the punishment side of it as well. So as we've mentioned earlier, as you are increasing your budgets things may not always go to plan. Be in a position to either not increase but also to be able to decrease your budget and using that as a signal for Facebook that something isn't quite working as you need it to. So let me give you an example. This is a client which we took on and the goal was to get to 10k per day within 30 days and target a CPA of $25. Within this example we had a proven product, we had a great landing page and we need to go and find the audiences that we're going to convert and convert well for this particular case. So this example here just to kind of show you how we incremented the spend up. So we started on a fairly low spend in the kind of hundreds of dollars per day and built that up to 600, 650 etc etc slowly scaling it up as we started to see that performance come in and then being more aggressive with our vertical scaling and majority of this was through vertical scaling to be able to react to good performance and to kind of bring the budget up as well. So here you can see for example the cost per website purchase with our goal being $25, $35, $25.53. One of the key things about this account is we are also doing one-time offers post-purchase as well. So for every purchase we bring in usually there's a second purchase as well. So the Ryus is even better than we're recording here because this screen within Facebook in the account overview only shows one conversion event here which is for us website purchases. So actually the amount spent in the website purchase conversion is a lot higher because we have a post-purchase goal as well and this is something I massively, massively recommend for any commerce business is to think about the lifetime value and that's obviously further down the pipeline but also think about another sale as soon as they make that initial purchase which is working really well for this client as well. And so you can see here for example as we start to scale up and we're going up here in terms of our CPAs $37.33 and we're doing optimizations and bringing that down to $24.27 which with the second purchase is even lower so we're looking at $15-$20 cost per acquisition for this particular account and at the same time we're scaling the spend up so we've hit four and a half thousand here but one of the key things to mention is as we started to see that the CPA was drifting I've actually gone in and reduced that spend down and then started to increase it when things are going well. So when you're looking to scale up don't be constantly looking for that line to be going into the top right sometimes if performance isn't good you bring it down and as we saw with the performance on this account the CPA did improve and so this is something that you have to keep in mind as you're going through that scaling strategy to be able to move
